original_hypothesis.title: Flash Loan ERC20 Missing Balance Delta Verification Enables Token Drain with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan perform raw safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom without pre/post balance checks. Unlike erc20TransferInLiquidity and erc20TransferOutLiquidity which verify balance deltas, flash loan transfers do not validate that the exact amount was received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the pool loses value on each flash loan (fee deducted on both outgoing and incoming transfer).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states support for fee-on-transfer tokens. Other transfer functions in VaultLogic have balance delta checks, but flash loan transfers do not. This creates a slow drain vector through repeated flash loans.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as a pool asset with flash loan enabled
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Project documentation states 'ERC20 (Any implementation, explicitly including weird traits: fee-on-transfer)'. No deployment evidence of specific fee-on-transfer tokens configured.

proof_of_signal.pattern: erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan and erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan lack pre/post balance checks present in erc20TransferInLiquidity (lines 300-310)

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as pool asset
Flash loan is enabled for that token
Token charges fee on both transfer directions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No balanceOf checks before/after transfers unlike other transfer functions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For 1% fee-on-transfer: flash loan 1000, receiver gets 990, returns 1000 but pool receives 990. Pool loses 10 per cycle.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing pre/post balance checks, missing require(poolSizeAfter >= poolSizeBefore)

analysis_questions: Which configured tokens have fee-on-transfer behavior?
How many flash loan cycles to drain significant liquidity?
Does the pool's tracked availableLiquidity become inconsistent with actual balance?
validation_requirements: Verify pool balance decreases after flash loan round-trip
Calculate drain rate for realistic fee percentages

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Pool has 10000 FEE_TOKEN (1% transfer fee). 2) Attacker flash loans 10000. Pool sends 10000, attacker receives 9900. 3) Attacker approves and returns 10000. Pool receives 9900. 4) Pool lost 200 tokens total (2 * 1% * 10000). 5) Repeat.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gradual drain of fee-on-transfer token liquidity through repeated flash loans
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires disabling flash loan for affected tokens

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No flash loan test with fee-on-transfer tokens

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be repeated rapidly in multiple transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Pool balance decreasing without corresponding state changes

validation_result.mechanism: Missing balance delta verification on flash loan transfers
validation_result.impact_summary: Flash loan transfers for fee-on-transfer tokens lack pre/post balance checks present in other transfer functions, causing the pool to lose tokens on each flash loan cycle and violating the solvency invariant.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis identifies a genuine code defect in VaultLogic.sol where `erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan` and `erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan` perform raw safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom without verifying the actual received/sent amounts via balance delta checks. This contrasts with `erc20TransferInLiquidity`, `erc20TransferOutLiquidity`, `erc20TransferInBidAmount`, `erc20TransferOutBidAmount`, and `erc20TransferBetweenWallets` — ALL of which include pre/post balance checks with `require(poolSizeAfter == (poolSizeBefore + amount))`. For a fee-on-transfer token with f% fee: (1) Pool sends X tokens out, receiver gets X*(1-f). (2) Receiver returns X tokens, pool receives X*(1-f). Pool's actual balance decreased by X*f, but availableLiquidity is never modified during flash loans, creating an accounting discrepancy. While the attacker also loses X*f tokens (to cover the return transfer fee), this is a griefing vector that gradually drains pool balance below tracked availableLiquidity, eventually causing legitimate withdrawals to fail. The project explicitly claims support for fee-on-transfer tokens, yet the flash loan code path lacks the defensive balance checks used everywhere else.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct comparison of transfer functions in VaultLogic.sol proves the defect. erc20TransferInLiquidity includes `uint256 poolSizeBefore = IERC20Upgradeable(asset).balanceOf(address(this))` and `require(poolSizeAfter == (poolSizeBefore + amount))`. erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan has no such checks. erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan similarly lacks checks. The executeFlashLoanERC20 function in FlashLoanLogic.sol also has no overall pre/post balance assertion to ensure pool solvency is maintained after the flash loan round-trip.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No pre/post balance check. For fee-on-transfer tokens, pool receives less than amounts[i] but no verification catches this. Pool actual balance becomes less than tracked availableLiquidity.
No pre/post balance check on outgoing transfer. For fee-on-transfer tokens, pool deducts amounts[i] but receiver gets less, and the deficit is never detected.
This function correctly verifies balance deltas. Fee-on-transfer tokens would cause this require to fail, protecting the pool. The flash loan equivalents lack this protection.
No pre/post balance check wrapping the entire flash loan flow. Should verify balanceOf(this) >= preBalance after the round-trip to ensure no tokens were lost to transfer fees.

calling_sequence: executeFlashLoanERC20 → VaultLogic.erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan (sends tokens to receiver, NO balance check)
executeFlashLoanERC20 → VaultLogic.erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan (pulls tokens back from receiver, NO balance check)
Flash loan completes. Pool actual balance < pre-flash-loan balance for fee-on-transfer tokens.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The flash loan flow transfers tokens out via erc20TransferOutOnFlashLoan which uses raw safeTransfer without balance delta verification. After the callback, erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan pulls tokens back via raw safeTransferFrom, again without balance delta verification. For a fee-on-transfer token with 1% fee and a flash loan of 10000 tokens: pool sends 10000 (receiver gets 9900), receiver sends 10000 back (pool receives 9900). Pool lost 100 tokens. The availableLiquidity is never modified during flash loans, so after multiple cycles, actual balance falls below tracked availableLiquidity, violating INV-ECON-001 and INV-ECON-006.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as a pool asset with flash loan enabled
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (InitConfigPool.s.sol) configure WETH, USDT, and DAI — none are fee-on-transfer tokens. However, the Configurator pattern allows adding arbitrary assets post-deployment. Project documentation explicitly states support for fee-on-transfer tokens. No deployment script explicitly configures a fee-on-transfer token, but no script prohibits it either.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario in the hypothesis is partially correct but overstates the ease. The attacker must provide their own tokens to cover the return-transfer fee. For a 1% fee token: Attacker flash loans 10000, receives 9900, must return 10000 (needs 100 extra tokens of their own), pool receives 9900. Pool lost 100, attacker lost 100. This is a griefing attack (not free drain), but the code defect is real. Over repeated flash loans, pool balance diverges from tracked availableLiquidity. Eventually, other users' withdrawals fail because actual balance < availableLiquidity. The attacker can amplify this with large flash loan amounts — flash loaning the entire available liquidity causes maximum damage per cycle.
validation_result.impact_assessment: For any fee-on-transfer token configured with flash loans enabled: (1) Each flash loan cycle causes the pool's actual token balance to decrease by fee_percentage * flash_loan_amount. (2) The tracked availableLiquidity remains unchanged, creating an accounting discrepancy. (3) After sufficient cycles, the pool becomes insolvent for that token — withdrawals and borrows will revert. (4) All depositors of that token lose access to the fee-deducted portion. The severity depends entirely on whether fee-on-transfer tokens are actually configured, which the project claims to support.
recommended_mitigations: Add pre/post balance checks in executeFlashLoanERC20: record balanceOf before the outgoing transfer, verify balanceOf after the incoming transfer is >= the pre-balance. Example: `uint256[] memory preBalances; for each asset: preBalances[i] = IERC20(assets[i]).balanceOf(address(this));` then after erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan: `require(IERC20(assets[i]).balanceOf(address(this)) >= preBalances[i])`.
Alternatively, modify erc20TransferInOnFlashLoan to perform balance delta verification similar to erc20TransferInLiquidity, reverting if the received amount is less than expected.
Consider disabling flash loans entirely for fee-on-transfer tokens via a configuration flag, or adding a flash loan premium that covers potential fee losses.
Add a protocol-level invariant check that actual token balance >= availableLiquidity + totalBidAmount, callable by anyone to detect discrepancies.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer ERC20 (e.g., 1% fee on every transfer). 2. Configure it as a pool asset with flash loans enabled. 3. Deposit 10000 tokens into the pool. 4. Deploy a FlashLoanReceiver contract that approves the pool and returns the requested amount. 5. Execute a flash loan for 10000 tokens. 6. Assert that pool's actual balance < 10000 (should be ~9900). 7. Assert that assetData.availableLiquidity is still 10000 (unchanged). 8. Repeat flash loan multiple times and observe compounding balance loss.
uncertainties: Whether fee-on-transfer tokens will actually be deployed in production pools. The deployment scripts only show standard tokens (WETH, USDT, DAI).
The exact fee-on-transfer behavior (one-way vs bidirectional fee, percentage, etc.) affects the magnitude of the drain.
Whether the Configurator has any additional checks when adding assets that might prevent fee-on-transfer tokens from being configured with flash loans enabled.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test flash loans with various fee-on-transfer token mock implementations to verify balance discrepancy.
Test the maximum drain rate per transaction for realistic fee percentages (0.1%, 1%, 5%).
Verify whether any other code paths (besides flash loans) are also vulnerable to fee-on-transfer issues despite the balance checks (e.g., edge cases in bid amount transfers).
Test whether the protocol's Configurator allows setting isFlashLoanEnabled for arbitrary ERC20 assets.
